settings.subtitle: The Gut-Skin Axis
settings.heading: Your dermatologist is treating the wrong organ.
settings.text: Acne, eczema, and psoriasis are not skin diseases. They are inflammatory conditions driven by a broken gut barrier. When ancestral bacteria deplete — a documented pattern in immigrant and diaspora communities — your intestinal wall leaks endotoxins into your bloodstream. Your immune system responds. Your skin pays the price. No serum, steroid, or retinoid can fix what starts below the surface.

settings.heading: You've tried everything on your skin. Nobody has tried fixing what's underneath it.

settings.text:
The topical spiral: cleanser, toner, serum, retinoid, moisturizer, SPF — and when that fails, prescription steroids, antibiotics, Accutane consultations. Every product suppresses a symptom for weeks, then the flare returns worse. You rotate products like a desperate scientist running out of hypotheses, because you've been solving the wrong equation entirely.

The $200/month skincare graveyard: a bathroom cabinet full of half-used bottles that promised 'clear skin in 30 days.' CeraVe. La Roche-Posay. Drunk Elephant. Prescription tretinoin. LED masks. Chemical peels. You've spent thousands on an industry that profits from your skin never actually clearing. The global acne treatment market is $12 billion. They don't make money when you're cured.

The covering up: foundation over eczema patches. Long sleeves in summer. Cancelling plans because your face is flaring. The psychological weight of skin conditions isn't vanity — it's exhaustion. You've rearranged your entire life around an organ that's screaming a message from your gut, and nobody told you to listen to the right organ.

settings.button_text: Clear Your Skin From the Inside — $34/mo

settings.text: The Science
The gut-skin axis: your skin is a billboard for your gut's dysfunction.

Research across three landmark studies confirms the same mechanism: disrupted gut microbiome composition leads to increased intestinal permeability, systemic endotoxemia, and inflammatory cytokine cascades that manifest as skin disease. Patients with acne show significantly altered gut flora compared to controls. Eczema patients have measurably higher intestinal permeability. Psoriasis correlates with specific bacterial depletions. L. plantarum and L. acidophilus directly reduce IL-6, TNF-alpha, and IL-17 — the exact inflammatory markers elevated in all three conditions. When you seal the gut, the skin signal resolves.

Salem et al., Journal of Dermatological Science, 2019 · Bowe & Logan, Gut Pathogens, 2011 · De Pessemier et al., Microorganisms, 2021

settings.subtitle: How It Works
settings.heading: Three steps from leaky gut to clear skin.

settings.title: Inflammation floods your bloodstream
settings.text: When ancestral bacteria deplete — accelerated by immigration, westernized diets, and antibiotic exposure — intestinal tight junctions weaken. Lipopolysaccharides (endotoxins) leak through the gut wall into systemic circulation. Your immune system detects the breach and mounts a full inflammatory response. The inflammation needs an exit point. Your skin is the largest organ. It becomes the battlefield.

settings.title: Every flare is an internal fire reaching the surface
settings.text: Circulating inflammatory cytokines — IL-6, TNF-alpha, IL-17 — activate mast cells and keratinocytes in your skin. Sebum production spikes (acne). Epidermal barrier function collapses (eczema). Keratinocytes hyper-proliferate (psoriasis). Your dermatologist sees the symptom and prescribes a topical. But the topical can't reach your gut. The flare subsides temporarily, then returns.
settings.source: Step 02 — Skin Becomes the Exit Wound

settings.title: Stop the leak. Stop the flare. Keep your skin clear.
settings.text: L. acidophilus restores tight junction proteins that seal the intestinal barrier. L. plantarum slashes the inflammatory cytokines that drive skin immune activation. Within weeks, systemic inflammation measurably drops. Flares decrease in frequency first, then intensity, then duration — until your skin reaches a baseline you haven't seen since before your microbiome shifted.

settings.heading: What skin that isn't under attack feels like.
Wake up and skip the mirror scan
settings.text: That anxious first look every morning — checking for new breakouts, scanning for flare-ups, bracing for what today's skin will be. When gut-driven inflammation drops, your skin stabilizes. You stop checking because there's nothing to find.

settings.heading: Throw out the 10-step routine
settings.text: Cleanser, toner, essence, serum, treatment, eye cream, moisturizer, SPF, primer, concealer. When the internal cause resolves, a simple cleanser and moisturizer become genuinely enough. Your bathroom shelf clears out. Your morning takes 3 minutes, not 20.

settings.heading: Bare skin in public without thinking twice
settings.text: Short sleeves over arms that used to have eczema patches. A bare face at brunch. A swimsuit without strategizing which spots to cover. When your skin clears from the inside, you stop performing confidence and start actually having it.

settings.question: How does fixing my gut clear my skin?
settings.answer: Acne, eczema, and psoriasis are inflammatory skin conditions — not surface problems. The inflammation driving them often originates in a leaky gut. When bacterial populations deplete and the intestinal barrier weakens, bacterial endotoxins (LPS) enter your bloodstream. Your immune system launches an inflammatory response that manifests in the skin through elevated IL-6, TNF-alpha, and IL-17. Origin's strains — particularly L. acidophilus and L. plantarum — restore gut barrier integrity and directly reduce these inflammatory cytokines. When the gut stops leaking, the skin stops flaring.

settings.question: How long before I see improvement in my skin?
settings.answer: Most people notice reduced frequency of flares within 4-6 weeks. The inflammatory cascade takes time to resolve as the gut barrier strengthens and systemic inflammation decreases. Eczema patches often begin fading within 3-4 weeks. Acne frequency and severity typically improve over 4-8 weeks. Psoriasis plaques — which are driven by a more complex immune pathway — often show improvement from 6 weeks onward. The direction of change is consistent; the timeline varies by individual microbiome composition and the severity of barrier disruption.

settings.question: Can I keep using my topical treatments while taking Origin?
settings.answer: Yes. There's no conflict between Origin and topical skincare, prescription treatments, or medications. Continue your current skincare routine while Origin works internally. Many customers find they need topical products less — and eventually stop flaring — as the internal inflammation resolves. Do not discontinue prescription medications without consulting your physician, especially immunosuppressants or biologics.

settings.question: Why did my acne or eczema get worse after moving countries?
settings.answer: Immigration and dietary westernization accelerate bacterial depletion — specifically the strains that maintain gut barrier integrity. This is a documented pattern in diaspora communities. When the gut barrier weakens, inflammatory triggers that previously stayed in your intestines begin entering systemic circulation. Your skin — as the body's largest detoxification organ — absorbs this inflammatory load. Skin conditions that were absent or mild in your home country worsening after migration is a direct signal of microbiome disruption.

settings.question: Is the gut-skin connection scientifically established?
settings.answer: Yes. The gut-skin axis is well-documented in peer-reviewed research. Salem et al. (Journal of Dermatological Science, 2019) demonstrated altered gut microbiome in acne patients. Bowe and Logan (Gut Pathogens, 2011) documented the gut-acne connection specifically in diaspora and immigrant populations. De Pessemier et al. (Microorganisms, 2021) reviewed the evidence across acne, eczema, and psoriasis, confirming that gut microbiome disruption precedes and drives all three conditions through shared inflammatory mechanisms.
